comments.text: i checked all 2^120 <n < 2^128 and an OEIS comment suggests all n<2^120 have been checked. i used this algorithm (its AI-generated but i doubt its wrong, the code logic is pretty simple). It uses divisibility constraints derived on the prime puzzles website and a primality test. for this range, the divisibility was enough to rule all of them out. The run between 2^127 and 2^128 took about an hour on an M5 macbook, so it shouldn´t be that hard to improve with better hardware.
This problem has been discussed extensively on https://www.primepuzzles.net/problems/prob_003.htm (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Very minor observation: here, any proof of infinitely many such $n$ would imply infinitely many twin primes, so affirmatively proving this appears at least as hard as the twin prime conjecture.
Since there are surely only finitely many such $n$, I doubt whether this problem is (in practice) comparable in difficulty to twin primes.
The usual heuristics suggests no, but If we replace "prime" with "squarefree", then one would expect a positive answer.
